lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly rent, collect, launder, inspect, repair, replace, inventory, and deliver table and bed linens, towels, diapers, and nonindustrial uniforms, gowns, or coats to external business customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Industrial work-uniform and protective-apparel launders classified in 812332, customer-owned garment cleaning, captive hospital or hospitality laundries, direct textile sales without a recurring service, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Healthcare, hospitality, food service, personal-care, and other businesses pay recurring rental and service charges, commonly under multi-year agreements with scheduled pickup and replacement routes.

a.bridge: O*NET's laundry-worker tasks and Vestis's recurring route and plant workflow are translated into linen-supply labor exposure. Customer service, contract and invoice review, demand forecasting, route planning, inventory reconciliation, order exceptions, machine-setting support, vision inspection, and staffing decisions are treated as AI-exposed; collection, loading, washing, drying, sorting, folding, repair, packing, delivery, and maintenance remain primarily physical. O*NET omits many route and office roles, so a production-only mapping likely biases total exposure downward.
a.rationale: Scaled linen routes generate abundant scheduling, inventory, account, quality, and logistics data, but the core service repeatedly moves and processes physical textiles. AI is more likely to augment plants and routes than remove most direct labor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted activity study across independent linen plants and routes separating already automated work from soil sorting, wash-floor operations, finishing, quality control, inventory, route delivery, service, sales, and administration.
caveats: O*NET spans consumer, institutional, industrial, and linen-supply laundry workers without industry wage weights.
Vestis mixes industrial uniforms, mats, supplies, and linen products and is much larger than the target population.
Healthcare and hospitality linen workflows have different contamination, quality, and exception requirements.

rho.bridge: Vestis reports digital customer portals, predictive analytics, route check-in improvements, strategic pricing tools, standardized operations, network optimization, and possible AI use. Those demonstrated data systems are converted into the five-year implemented share of exposed work at lower-middle-market linen suppliers. Vestis has national scale and dedicated technology investment, so the proxy likely biases implementation upward for smaller firms.
rho.rationale: Route density, tagged inventory, recurring orders, automated plant equipment, and stable accounts create favorable data and integration conditions. Capital cost, legacy machinery, cybersecurity, customer-specific handling rules, and limited technical teams slow adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Production telemetry from laundry ERP, RFID, route, vision-inspection, and plant-automation vendors showing adoption, override rates, missorts, route miles, labor hours removed, service failures, and payback by operator size.
caveats: Digital portals and predictive analytics are not direct measures of implemented AI labor substitution.
Public-company systems and capital resources may not transfer to independent operators.
Customer integration, RFID coverage, and plant equipment age vary materially.

e.rationale: Linen supply is explicitly a recurring rental or contract service and therefore fits the default lens well. The eligible share is reduced for firms dominated by direct sales, captive or single-customer operations, owner dependence, nontransferable contracts, industrial-laundry misclassification, or economics outside the target band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 812331 census linking establishments to common ownership, normalized EBITDA, route density, contract term, customer concentration, plant capacity, linen versus industrial-uniform mix, and transferable permits and customer agreements.
caveats: Public filings combine 812331 linen with activities classified in 812332 and other service categories.
Plant, depot, route, and legal-entity counts can overstate distinct transferable firms.
The supplied firm estimate is margin-bridged and may not reflect capital-intensive plant economics.

s5.rationale: The market includes national consolidators and hundreds of smaller regional operators; UniFirst states that it regularly evaluates garment-service acquisitions. These observations establish a buyer channel but not a linen-specific transfer denominator or owner-succession rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction dataset from investment banks, laundry-industry brokers, PitchBook, and SEC filings, coded to 812331 and matched to eligible firms, seller ownership, route overlap, and customer-retention outcomes.
caveats: Public-company acquisition strategy does not quantify industry-wide completed transfers.
Deals may be classified as industrial uniform or facility services rather than linen supply.
No owner-age or succession distribution is observed for eligible firms.

q.bridge: Vestis reports primarily multi-year contracts, specified early-exit costs, digital strategic-pricing work, and 91.8% fiscal-2025 recurring-business retention. Those broad uniform-and-workplace-supply contract characteristics are translated into the discounted share of linen-supply automation benefit retained through renewal. Because large route providers have scale, bundled products, and contractual leverage beyond independent linen firms, the proxy likely biases retention upward.
q.rationale: Contracted recurring rental fees can preserve plant and route savings before renewal, and reliable service has switching friction. Competitive bidding, customer procurement, transparent surcharges, service credits, labor-indexed pricing, and contract resets eventually share gains with customers.
q.change_evidence: Representative linen contracts and renewal cohorts showing term, escalation, exit charges, service credits, technology fees, automation savings, customer churn, and post-renewal unit economics.
caveats: The retention metric covers Vestis's broader recurring rental portfolio rather than 812331 alone.
Retention does not directly measure pass-through of automation benefits.
Healthcare and hospitality procurement may exert more price pressure than the blended public-company customer base.

d5.bridge: Vestis reports that demand depends on employment, hygiene and safety standards, and outsourcing; its fiscal-2025 revenue excluding an extra week declined 4.4%. UniFirst reports 1.7% normalized organic growth in its uniform and facility-services segment. These divergent public-company revenue observations are converted into a nearly flat five-year constant-quality linen-service quantity base. Both companies mix industrial uniforms and ancillary products, and nominal pricing is included, so using their blended revenues likely overstates linen quantity growth.
d5.rationale: Healthcare, hospitality, food service, and hygiene needs sustain recurring textile volume, and outsourcing can expand. Employment cycles, reuse reduction, disposable substitutes, customer insourcing, and route competition keep the base close to flat rather than extrapolating nominal revenue.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year pounds processed, pieces delivered, active wearer or bed equivalents, route stops, and contract starts and losses for independent linen suppliers, segmented by healthcare, hospitality, food service, and customer closures.
caveats: Nominal company revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality linen quantity.
Both public-company segments include activities outside 812331.
Customer employment and occupancy can diverge by served end market.

o.rationale: Software can optimize inventory, routes, pricing, and service, but rented linen still requires accountable ownership, collection, hygienic processing, quality assurance, replacement, and delivery. Customers can insource laundry, buy disposable products, or switch to direct ownership, yet software alone cannot supply the physical service.
o.change_evidence: Customer cohorts measuring insourcing, disposable substitution, direct-purchase conversion, fully automated plant labor, contamination incidents, linen loss, route exceptions, and service accountability after automation.
caveats: Operator intensity differs between commodity hospitality linen and regulated healthcare textiles.
Robotics and automated sorting could reduce plant labor without removing the linen-service operator.
Customers may change the service basket toward disposables or owned goods rather than software-only supply.

narrative.executive_view: Linen supply combines high labor intensity with recurring contracts, route data, and standardized processing. AI can improve inventory, routing, inspection, pricing, service, and plant coordination, but the physical collection, laundering, finishing, and delivery system remains central.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can forecast piece demand, reconcile tagged inventory, optimize routes and loads, detect stains or damage, recommend wash settings, automate invoices and service messages, predict churn, and assist plant staffing. Physical textile handling, sanitation, repair, packing, driving, and customer delivery remain substantial.
narrative.value_capture: Multi-year contracts and switching friction can preserve some efficiency benefit, while renewals, competitive bids, service credits, and procurement sophistication share gains with customers. Large incumbents likely retain more than small local suppliers.
narrative.firm_availability: The NAICS model fits recurring outsourced service, and public filings describe a fragmented market with active consolidators. Count quality remains uncertain because linen, industrial uniforms, ancillary products, plants, depots, and legal entities can be mixed.
narrative.demand_durability: Healthcare, hospitality, food service, hygiene standards, and outsourcing support recurring volume. Demand still follows customer employment and occupancy, and disposables, insourcing, contract loss, or service failure can reduce route density.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are linen-specific versus industrial-uniform mix, plant age, route density, labor availability, utility cost, textile loss, sanitation requirements, customer concentration, contract transferability, public-company proxy bias, and the absence of direct AI productivity studies.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring contracts, tagged inventory, dense routes, and standardized plant workflows create multiple implementable data-driven efficiency levers.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most payroll supports physical processing and delivery, while public-company evidence blends adjacent industries and may overstate small-operator technology readiness and contract leverage.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 81
sources.supports: Defines 812331 as contract or rental supply of laundered table and bed linens, towels, diapers, and specified nonindustrial uniforms or gowns, and distinguishes industrial launderers.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Laundry and Dry-Cleaning Workers
sources.supports: Lists loading, machine operation, sorting, counting, marking, inspection, stain treatment, pressing, folding, packaging, equipment cleaning, and other physical laundry tasks.

sources.title: Vestis Corporation Fiscal 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes recurring weekly pickup and replacement routes, multi-year contracts and exit costs, fragmented competition, digital portals, predictive analytics, pricing and network tools, demand drivers, and a 4.4% adjusted fiscal-2025 revenue decline.

sources.title: Vestis Fourth Quarter Fiscal 2025 Earnings Presentation
sources.supports: Reports 91.8% fiscal-2025 rolling recurring-business retention and describes strategic pricing, standardized operations, logistics optimization, and planned cost savings.

sources.title: UniFirst Corporation Fiscal 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes weekly textile-service routes, written service contracts, a competitive market with hundreds of smaller businesses, regular acquisition evaluation, technology investment, and 1.7% normalized organic growth in uniform and facility services.
